quotedTweet.text:
I used to be in charge of all the crude and product tank inventories in the refinery. 

Here is a simple picture of the operating levels of the tank. You've got deadstocks at the bottom of the tank because the pump nozzle is positioned slightly above a certain level. We used to call this 'the unpumpables'. Go below this level and your feed pump would cavitate. Big no-no.

Then you get a minimum working level after catering for contingency levels (e.g. enough product stocks to cater for x days of unplanned shutdown).

And although you have a huge tank that can technically fill to the brim, the actual working volume at the top is lower than the absolute max to also factor in for contingency levels (e.g. enough capacity to store off-spec rundown for an unplanned shutdown). Tank top scenario is a horror nobody wants to face due to risk of loss of primary containment (i.e. a safety incident).

In the current low stocks environment in the US, you are going to 'scrape tank bottoms', up to the minimum working level. So you will always have some stocks  because you can't draw to absolute zero.

Every single drop of Russian grain coming out of the Sea of Azov has to bottle through the Kerch Strait, mostly for transshipment plays.

Since the draft is too shallow for deep-sea vessels to enter those ports directly, small coasters are the only ones getting through. 

Everything else coming out of Azov has to hit the Kavkaz anchorage on the Black Sea side of the strait for transshipment before making the final run to destination markets.

We are seeing a dislocation at a chokepoint that moves a full third of Russian wheat exports. Ukraine is aggressively hammering Crimea and the Azov axis, and the logistics network is fracturing.

Just last weekend, the tape showed a solid queue of waiting vessels and active STS at the Kavkaz anchorage. Fast forward to this weekend, and every single vessel has completely vanished from the coordinates like a mirage.

Zelensky is putting a serious squeeze on Putin’s pain points.

COT on #commodities covering the week to 14 July captured a broad improvement in risk appetite across the sector, with the Bloomberg Commodity Total Return Index (BCOM) rising 3.1%, led by a powerful rally in energy (excluding natural gas), alongside renewed strength in copper, soybean oil and wheat.

Energy positioning saw the biggest shift. The net long in Brent crude more than doubled to almost 170,000 contracts as an aggressive bout of short covering combined with fresh long buying following the renewed escalation of Middle East tensions. Together with additional buying in WTI, the combined speculative net long climbed to around 240,000 contracts, the highest in five weeks, although still well below the peaks seen earlier this year. In contrast, the 11% weekly slump in US natural gas encouraged fresh bearish positioning, with the net short widening to more than 50,000 contracts, the largest in 27 months.

Across metals, gold attracted modest net buying despite a weekly price decline, highlighting continued strategic interest from money managers. Silver sentiment softened, with the net long reduced by 14%, while copper buying resumed after several weeks of position trimming, lifting the net long by 3% as tightening visible inventories and renewed optimism over industrial demand improved sentiment.

The grain sector also experienced a notable shift in positioning. Buying was driven primarily by short covering as Middle East and renewed Black Sea tensions and weather concerns forced traders to reassess the outlook. Even after a 41% reduction in the net short, speculative funds remained meaningfully short CBOT wheat heading into the reporting week's close, leaving the market vulnerable to the sharp rally that unfolded in the days immediately afterwards as Black Sea export disruption fears intensified.

COT on #forex covering the week to 14 July showed speculative appetite for the US dollar beginning to ease after a sharp multi-week build-up drove the aggregate non-commercial long against eight IMM currency futures to a 10-year high above USD 40 billion. During the latest reporting week, that net long was trimmed by 4% to USD 39.3 billion, suggesting some early profit-taking rather than a decisive shift in sentiment.

The reduction was driven primarily by short covering in EUR, CHF and, most notably, GBP, and only partly offset by continued selling AUD and CAD. Despite the modest pullback, overall positioning remains heavily skewed in favour of the dollar, reflecting continued expectations that US interest rates will stay elevated relative to most other developed markets.

WEEKLY MACRO UPDATE — July 19, 2026

─────────────────

MARKETS

Russell 2000 3,150–3,200.

CNN Fear and Greed at 38, Fear.

─────────────────

KOSPI AND SOXX

The KOSPI closed at 6,820, down 9.69% on the week. 

SOXX closed at 521.81, down 7.67%. 

Both continue to show what happens after a parabolic structure breaks, and the KOSPI remains the clearest real-world case study of this entire cycle. Over 300,000 individual retail accounts in Korea have now been forcibly liquidated through this unwind, out of more than 1.2 million accounts that became subject to margin calls. Regulators there have since moved to restrict leveraged single-stock ETFs directly because of the role they played in amplifying the collapse.

SOXX is tracing a similar sequence with a multi-week lag. This lines up with the momentum data from Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley this week: the S&P 500 momentum factor suffered its sharpest three-week reversal since the early 2000s, concentrated almost entirely in the crowded long-semiconductors, short-hyperscalers trade. 

SOXX now has the structural potential to trace the same violent volatility the KOSPI is currently working through. That is not unusual. 

A generation of leveraged retail capital has learned to buy every dip, and this is the mechanism by which that gets shaken out. 

The KOSPI is effectively the mirror for what likely comes next for SOXX, then the Nasdaq, and probably crypto, over the coming weeks and months.

─────────────────

SECTOR ROTATION

XLE +3.24%, 
KRE +2.21%, 
XLRE +1.83% led. 

SOXX −7.67% and XLK −4.21% lagged sharply. 
IWM/SPY posted its first positive week in three, up approximately 1%.

Not a broad risk-off move. Capital is rotating out of the AI hardware trade and into energy, financials and real estate. The full breakdown follows in this week's Sector Risk Monitor.

─────────────────

INFLATION

The June CPI print delivered a genuine surprise. Headline CPI fell 0.4% month over month against an expectation of -0.1%, the largest monthly decline since April 2020, bringing the annual rate down from 4.2% to 3.5%. 

Core CPI was flat on the month versus an expected 0.2% rise, pulling the annual core rate down to 2.6% from 2.9%.

Almost the entire improvement came from energy. Energy fell 5.7%, gasoline dropped 9.7%, together accounting for roughly 0.4 percentage points of the headline decline. Strip that out and prices were essentially flat. Even the flat core number overstates the improvement: vehicle insurance fell 2.0%, wireless service fell 3.3%, hotel prices fell 2.3%, together removing about 0.13 percentage points from core CPI. The underlying trend was probably closer to a soft 0.1% to 0.2% monthly increase.

The pattern underneath matters more than the headline. Shelter is up 3.3% year over year, hospital services up 5.1%, vehicle maintenance up 7.0%, while durables, medical commodities and technology goods are flat or falling. That is a textbook late-cycle signature: pricing power disappearing wherever a purchase can be postponed, staying sticky wherever it cannot.

Markets took a visible breath of relief on this print, largely because the Fed had signaled a possible hike had inflation come in hot. Even if that had happened, it would have been the wrong policy response. This inflation cycle has been driven by a resource shock, a shortage from the Iran conflict and the Hormuz disruption, not a demand shock. 

Raising rates against a supply shock does not address the underlying cause.

─────────────────

INSIDER SELLING

Corporate insiders sold $77.6 billion of stock in the first half of 2026, up 20% year over year, the second-fastest pace in more than 20 years, exceeded only by 2021. 

This is being framed by many commentators as an imminent warning of collapse. That framing is too simple. In 2007, the insider sell/buy ratio hit a comparably extreme level, and the market continued rallying for roughly another 6 to 9 months before the real unwind began. Insiders distribute in tranches over many months, managing personal liquidity well ahead of the actual top rather than calling it precisely. 

This is a genuine medium to long-term warning sign, not evidence a collapse is imminent, and it needs to be read as its own separate data point.

─────────────────

STRUCTURAL FOMO

What makes this phase unusual is the near-total absence of classic euphoria. There is little mainstream coverage of what is actually happening in the KOSPI, or of the record volumes recently seen in SOXX and SOXL. That silence is itself informative. 

This is not a 1999-style hype cycle playing out across headlines. 

This is Structural FOMO: fifteen years of consistently rising markets have made being invested the default assumption rather than an active decision. It comes up directly in conversations. A client recently said he would simply "sit out" the next correction, without registering that what likely lies ahead does not resemble the corrections of the last several years, the only reference point that assumption is built on.

It shows up in the data too. Loss-making companies within the Russell 2000 are now being bought as aggressively as profitable ones. 

The number of listed leveraged ETF products in the US has exploded past 700, more than double the count at the end of 2024, with over 400 of them leveraged single-stock products. 

That behavior used to be almost exclusively associated with crypto retail trading and has now fully migrated into traditional markets, under the assumption that this is simply how markets work.

─────────────────

EARNINGS

Bank earnings last week were broadly strong: deposits growing, loan growth accelerating, trading and investment banking revenue up. Much of that strength reflects the parabolic market structure itself generating fee income rather than a healthier real economy underneath, and bank earnings are historically a lagging rather than leading indicator.

TSMC and ASML both delivered exceptional Q2 results this week, confirming the AI infrastructure buildout remains intact and, by some estimates, ahead of schedule.

The most important counterpoint came from IBM. On July 14, IBM issued a preliminary warning showing Q2 revenue of $17.2 billion against a consensus of roughly $17.86 billion, with adjusted EPS of $2.93 versus an expected $3.02. Shares fell 25.2%, the worst single-day decline in the company's history. 

CEO Arvind Krishna attributed the miss directly to clients redirecting budgets away from IBM's software and infrastructure offerings toward AI hardware, servers, storage and memory chips, amid ongoing supply constraints. This is a live, negative confirmation of the rotation thesis playing out inside a single earnings report, raising the question of whether this is company-specific or the beginning of a broader pattern. IBM's full Q2 results and Q3 guidance follow July 22.

BlackRock posted the strongest quarter in its history this week: AUM at $15.34 trillion, up $1.44 trillion in a single quarter, record inflows of $192 billion for the quarter and $321 billion for the first half. That is the Structural FOMO thesis expressed in numbers, capital being deployed continuously and at scale as everyone moves back into markets in Q2.

Week ahead: 

Tesla and Alphabet report July 22, Intel July 23, Microsoft July 29, IBM's full results and Q3 guidance July 22, alongside a wide cross-section of the real economy including Domino's, Union Pacific, Lockheed Martin, Honeywell, Comcast and Norfolk Southern.

─────────────────

RATES, DOLLAR AND COMMODITIES

10-year at 4.549%, −0.79% wk. 
2-year at 4.183%, −1.18% wk. 
1-year at 4.004%, −2.48% wk. 

Yields eased slightly but remain sticky at elevated levels despite the softer CPI print. My read is that this is less about the bond market believing the economy is strong, and more about structural fiscal concerns and geopolitical caution. 

If growth optimism were really driving yields, equities would likely be pushing higher in sync rather than sitting in rotation and consolidation. What is more likely priced in is a persistent term premium tied to ongoing deficit financing, largely independent of the current cycle stage, combined with a fresh geopolitical risk premium.

That risk premium showed up directly this week. Oil jumped 10.98% on renewed Iran tension over the weekend, a sharp reversal after weeks of decline. That kind of geopolitical oil spike tends to move short-term inflation expectations and yields even when the underlying disinflationary trend remains intact, which is exactly what the June CPI data showed.

Dollar at 100.75, −0.28% wk. 
Gold at 4,017, −2.18% wk. 
Silver at 55.911, −5.70% wk, both continuing to correct from their January peaks. 
VIX at 18.76, +14.95% wk.

The takeaway: sticky yields here look more like caution and fiscal premium than a signal that the market sees a strong, demand-driven economy. That distinction matters, because treating this as demand-side strength would argue for higher rates for longer. Treating it as a geopolitical and fiscal premium on top of an economy that is still fundamentally softening is a different read entirely, and the one the broader data continues to support.

─────────────────

CRYPTO

Bitcoin at approximately 64,600, +1.4% on the week. Ethereum at approximately 1,871, +3.6%. 

BTC outperformed the S&P by 3.14 percentage points this week. 

Crypto Fear and Greed at 35, Fear. 

Sentiment in crypto remains deeply bearish, with many comparing the current mood to something worse than the FTX collapse. 

Technically that comparison does not hold. Emotionally, for most participants, it probably does. 

This is close to a mirror image of what traditional markets are experiencing on the other side right now, and the bullish divergence between the two continues to widen. 

Crypto is unlikely to complete its four-year cycle the way most currently expect. It should instead benefit as that divergence closes, as part of the final rotation into breadth and, ultimately, into the last risk assets still lagging.

─────────────────

GEOPOLITICS

Iran tensions escalated again over the weekend, yet markets showed limited direct reaction. This phase is being widely misread right now, with many concluding that the market is topping out simply because it has already risen so strongly. There is currently no clear evidence for that. In this kind of market structure, momentum needs to be weighted more heavily than individual macro parameters, geopolitics included.

Wall Street is also increasingly focused on Russia. One of its largest logistics hubs near Moscow was struck over the weekend, adding to the ongoing campaign against refineries and energy infrastructure. Gasoline shortages, crop disruption risk and resource consolidation are all compounding. If this trajectory continues, the causal chain into winter, particularly around food and fuel, becomes genuinely critical to watch.

─────────────────

BOTTOM LINE

This was an extensive update, and it is worth stepping back. The current macro environment is not translating one to one into price the way markets have grown accustomed to watching more closely again in recent months, after years where headlines barely mattered. 

The structure has taken on a shape where momentum currently outweighs the influence of individual macro parameters. That showed clearly this week: markets took a visible breath of relief on the softer CPI print, even though the Fed had signaled a possible hike had inflation come in hot. Even if that had happened, it would have been the wrong policy response, reacting to a resource shock, a shortage, not a demand shock.

Markets remain in rotation mode and are moving into breadth. This is not a broad selloff of the entire market. It is a rotation of capital. The AI trade is being partially exited, and SOXX is visibly absorbing that pressure. SOXX now has the structural potential to trace the same violent volatility the KOSPI is currently working through, which is a normal consequence of a generation of leveraged retail capital that has learned to buy every dip finally getting shaken out. The KOSPI is the mirror for what likely comes next for SOXX, then the Nasdaq, and probably crypto, over the coming weeks and months.

Traditional markets remain in preparation for what is very likely their final leg higher. Once that breakout confirms, crypto deserves close attention. Sentiment in crypto remains deeply bearish. 

What often gets missed here is that traditional markets are already in an unambiguous risk-on posture. 

The Russell remains strong and shows no sign of wanting to go lower, only of wanting to continue. Loss-making companies inside it are being bought as aggressively as profitable ones. 

The Equal Weight index continues confirming genuine breadth. Anyone waiting for a moment of maximum euphoria, front-page headlines, mainstream coverage everywhere, may get exactly that in the final one to two months of this move. The more likely path in the meantime is quieter: buying and leveraging that continues as a matter of course, with a large share of retail only becoming fully aware and piling into these products right near the top.

The coming period will be spent focused more closely on corrective structures, both to prepare portfolios and refine risk management ahead of what follows. The expectation remains that crypto sees another run, a fundamentally different one from anything experienced in this asset class so far. 

The distinction between linear, mid-cycle behavior and parabolic, exponential cycles has been a recurring theme here, and equities are now demonstrating exactly that distinction in real time. Crypto has always been macro-dependent. There is no coherent reason equities would run parabolic and exponential while crypto continues along a purely linear, self-contained cycle of its own.

MARKET UPDATE — FRIDAY, 17 JULY 2026

The AI trade is facing its most serious test of the year.

Chip stocks are tumbling across Asia, Nasdaq futures are sharply lower and investors are questioning whether enormous spending on AI infrastructure can justify valuations that had already assumed years of exceptional growth.

At the same time, renewed US–Iran fighting is pushing oil higher and reviving inflation fears—just as several Federal Reserve officials begin discussing the possibility of raising interest rates again.

This is not one isolated sell-off.

It is a collision between expensive technology stocks, forced position unwinding, higher energy risk and a more hawkish Fed.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 MARKETS: THE CHIP ROUT DEEPENS

US stock futures point to another lower opening:

🔸 S&P 500 futures: −0.83%

🔸 Nasdaq 100 futures: −1.63%

🔸 Dow futures: −0.58%

🔸 VIX: 18.08, up 8.1%

The Nasdaq is leading the decline because semiconductor, memory and AI-infrastructure shares are once again under heavy pressure.

Nvidia, TSMC and Intel were all lower before the opening bell, while Sandisk, Western Digital, Micron, Applied Materials and other former momentum leaders continued to weaken.

The selling has spread globally.

🔸 Japan’s Nikkei 225 fell approximately 4%

🔸 Taiwan’s benchmark market dropped around 6.5%

🔸 China’s CSI 300 weakened approximately 3.6%

🔸 Europe’s Stoxx 600 fell, with ASML among the biggest technology casualties

South Korea’s market was closed for a holiday, but its semiconductor shares have already experienced severe volatility this month.

Simple explanation

Investors had crowded into the same group of AI and semiconductor companies because their prices kept rising.

Now that those shares are falling, traders using borrowed money, options and leveraged funds may be forced to sell—causing the decline to accelerate.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 SEMICONDUCTORS ARE APPROACHING A BEAR MARKET

The PHLX Semiconductor Index has fallen approximately 19% from its June record high, leaving it close to the 20% decline normally described as a bear market.

Micron has fallen around 30% from its June peak, while several memory and storage companies have lost more than 30% during the past month.

The sector is also heading for its worst week since the tariff-driven market rout of April 2025.

This is happening despite strong results from companies including TSMC and ASML.

TSMC reported a sharp rise in profits, but its shares still fell after investors focussed on plans for another $100 billion of US investment and questioned whether the industry is building too much capacity too quickly.

Simple explanation

Good earnings are no longer enough.

Investors are asking whether companies can earn a satisfactory return on the hundreds of billions being spent on AI chips, data centres and power infrastructure.

When a stock falls despite excellent results, it often means expectations had become even stronger than the actual performance.

Why it matters

The semiconductor sector may still deliver extraordinary long-term growth.

But markets do not value a company only on whether its profits are growing. They also consider how much future growth is already reflected in the share price.

The current sell-off is a reminder that a brilliant business can still be a poor investment when bought at an excessive valuation.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 A MOMENTUM CRASH—NOT YET A BROAD MARKET COLLAPSE

The pressure is most intense among stocks that had risen the fastest.

Goldman Sachs’ high-beta momentum basket has fallen roughly one-third from its peak, while another momentum index has dropped sharply since the technology rally peaked in June.

The decline appears to involve a combination of:

🔸 profit-taking after enormous gains

🔸 forced selling by leveraged investors

🔸 quantitative funds reversing momentum positions

🔸 concern about excessive AI spending

🔸 growing doubts about semiconductor valuations

Yet the broader market has held up better than the headline indexes suggest.

On Thursday, the S&P 500 fell 0.51%, but eight of its 11 sectors rose and the equal-weighted S&P 500 gained nearly 1%.

Financials, healthcare, consumer staples, retailers and transportation companies have recently outperformed many technology stocks.

Simple explanation

The average headline index looks weak because its largest technology companies carry enormous weight.

Underneath the surface, money is not leaving every stock. It is moving from expensive AI winners into other parts of the economy.

Why it matters

This is currently better described as a violent market rotation than a full-scale market breakdown.

However, the risk would increase if heavily leveraged technology investors were forced to sell profitable holdings in other sectors simply to cover losses.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 VOLATILITY IS RISING BENEATH THE SURFACE

The VIX rose above 18, reflecting increasing demand for protection against further market declines.

That remains below the level of 20 commonly associated with heightened fear, but the direction is important.

An even stronger warning comes from the unusually wide gap between the volatility of individual S&P 500 companies and the volatility of the index itself.

Individual shares are moving violently even though the overall index remains close to its record.

Simple explanation

The market index looks relatively calm because gains in some companies are offsetting losses elsewhere.

But underneath that calm surface, individual stocks are swinging sharply.

It resembles a lake that looks still from a distance while powerful currents are moving underneath.

Why it matters

Periods of extremely high disagreement between individual stocks can resolve harmlessly through rotation.

But they can also become dangerous if selling spreads from one crowded sector into the wider market.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 THURSDAY’S CLOSE: TECHNOLOGY HID A STRONGER UNDERLYING MARKET

US markets finished lower on Thursday:

🔸 Dow: −0.20% to 52,552.97

🔸 S&P 500: −0.51% to 7,533.77

🔸 Nasdaq Composite: −1.47% to 25,881.95

The equal-weighted S&P 500 ETF, which gives every company similar importance, rose approximately 1%.

That contrast reveals the real story.

The market was not uniformly weak. A relatively small group of enormous technology companies dragged down indexes even while most sectors advanced.

This broadening could eventually make the bull market healthier—but only if weakness in the AI trade remains contained.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 NETFLIX FALLS AS GROWTH SLOWS

Netflix shares dropped approximately 9% in premarket trading after the company forecast slower third-quarter growth and narrowed its full-year revenue outlook.

Second-quarter revenue rose 13% to $12.6 billion, helped by higher subscription prices and growing advertising revenue.

However, Netflix expects third-quarter revenue growth of approximately 11.7%, which would be its slowest expansion in around three years.

The company also plans to publish less viewing data.

Simple explanation

Netflix is still growing and making strong profits.

The problem is that investors had expected even faster growth.

When a company is valued as a rapid-growth business, merely meeting expectations may not be enough to support its share price.

Why it matters

Netflix’s decline reflects the same challenge affecting semiconductor companies:

Strong companies are being punished because their share prices had left little room for disappointment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 SPACEX FALLS BELOW ITS IPO PRICE

SpaceX closed Thursday at $131.11, below its reported $135 IPO price for the first time.

The shares weakened further after the 13th Starship test was aborted less than one second before launch when several engines did not start correctly.

The company plans to replace two engines and attempt another launch.

Simple explanation

The rocket’s safety system detected a problem and stopped the launch before liftoff.

That is better than allowing a damaged rocket to launch.

Why it matters

Starship is central to several parts of the SpaceX investment case:

🔸 deploying larger Starlink satellites

🔸 lowering launch costs

🔸 carrying heavier payloads

🔸 lunar and deep-space missions

🔸 potential orbital computing infrastructure

A successful launch would restore confidence.

Continued technical delays would affect nearly every major growth assumption behind the company’s valuation.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 ALPHABET FACES FRESH AI QUESTIONS

Alphabet shares fell more than 4% on Thursday following reports that Google’s Gemini 3.5 Pro model is months behind schedule because of performance problems.

At the same time, rivals in the US and China continue releasing new models.

China’s Moonshot AI introduced its Kimi-K3 model, which reportedly performed strongly in independent model comparisons and contributed to renewed concern that cheaper Chinese systems could challenge expensive US-developed models.

Simple explanation

AI leadership is changing quickly.

A company can spend billions building the best model, only for a competitor to release something stronger or cheaper a few months later.

Why it matters

This uncertainty makes it harder for investors to calculate how long today’s AI infrastructure will remain valuable—and whether the enormous spending behind it will earn acceptable returns.

The technology may transform the economy while still producing disappointing returns for some investors.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 BONDS: YIELDS FALL, BUT THE FED BECOMES MORE HAWKISH

Treasury yields moved lower this morning:

🔸 2-year Treasury: 4.126%, down 1.9 basis points

🔸 10-year Treasury: 4.521%, down 3.6 basis points

🔸 30-year Treasury: 5.055%, down 3.1 basis points

Falling yields would normally suggest investors expect weaker growth or easier monetary policy.

But today’s picture is more complicated.

Several Federal Reserve officials delivered hawkish messages.

🔶 Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan said modestly higher interest rates may be needed because inflation remains too high and current policy may not be sufficiently restrictive.

🔶 Fed Vice-Chair Philip Jefferson indicated that rate increases should be considered if inflation fails to cool, while Kansas City Fed President Jeffrey Schmid also warned that inflation remains excessive.

Simple explanation

The Fed has been waiting for inflation to return sustainably towards 2%.

Recent inflation data improved, but higher oil prices could push petrol, transport and production costs upwards again.

Some officials therefore believe a small rate rise now may prevent a larger inflation problem later.

Why it matters

Markets still expect the Fed to leave rates unchanged at its July meeting.

However, the discussion is shifting from when rates will be cut towards whether another increase may eventually be necessary.

That change matters because higher rates reduce the present value of future profits—placing particular pressure on highly valued technology companies.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 THE US LABOUR MARKET REMAINS RESILIENT

Initial unemployment claims fell by 8,000 to 208,000, significantly below economists’ expectations.

Continuing claims also declined by 16,000 to approximately 1.805 million.

The data support the view that the US remains in a “slow hire, slow fire” labour market.

Companies are hiring cautiously, but widespread layoffs have not developed.

Simple explanation

Relatively few Americans are losing their jobs.

That is good for households and economic growth, but it also gives the Fed less urgency to reduce interest rates.

Why it matters

A resilient labour market strengthens the argument of hawkish Fed officials:

the economy may be capable of tolerating higher interest rates if inflation rises again.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 OIL RISES AS THE US–IRAN WAR WIDENS

Oil remains one of the market’s biggest risks.

Brent crude was trading around $84–$86 per barrel, while WTI was near $79–$81. Both benchmarks were heading for weekly gains of approximately 11%–12% as disruption around the Strait of Hormuz intensified.

The United States carried out a sixth consecutive night of strikes against Iran, targeting coastal surveillance, air-defence, logistics and maritime infrastructure.

Iran responded with missile and drone attacks against US-linked positions across the region and said oil and gas exports through the Strait of Hormuz would remain blocked while US attacks continued.

The conflict also widened geographically.

Iran carried out its first known strike on Syrian territory during the current war, claiming it targeted a US special-operations command centre near al-Tanf. A Syrian military source said the attack missed the base and caused no casualties or material damage.

Iranian attacks were also reported against US-linked facilities in Jordan, Bahrain, Kuwait and Qatar, while US strikes continued around Bandar Abbas, Qeshm Island and southern Iranian transport infrastructure.

Iran has also reportedly asked its Houthi allies to prepare to threaten the Red Sea route if Iranian power infrastructure is attacked.

Simple explanation

The Strait of Hormuz and the Red Sea are two of the world’s most important energy transport routes.

If both become unsafe, fewer oil and gas shipments can reach global markets.

Less supply normally means higher prices.

Why it matters

The wider the conflict spreads, the greater the risk of:

🔸 damage to energy infrastructure

🔸 disruption to shipping and aviation

🔸 attacks on US bases

🔸 involvement by regional allies

🔸 accidental escalation

Higher oil would also affect the wider economy:

🔸 petrol and transport costs rise

🔸 airlines and shipping companies face higher expenses

🔸 manufacturing becomes more costly

🔸 inflation may accelerate

🔸 central banks become less willing to cut rates

🔸 consumer spending may weaken

The biggest market risk is therefore not simply another military strike.

It is a prolonged regional disruption that keeps oil elevated long enough to feed into inflation, company costs and central-bank policy.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 THE MIDDLE EAST’S BORROWING COSTS ARE RISING

Bond investors are demanding their highest additional compensation since 2022 to lend to Middle Eastern governments.

Simple explanation

Investors consider lending to the region more dangerous because the war could affect government finances, oil exports, infrastructure and economic activity.

They therefore demand higher interest payments to accept that risk.

Why it matters

Rising borrowing costs can make it more expensive for governments and companies to refinance debt, fund infrastructure and support their economies.

The financial effects of the conflict are now spreading beyond oil into credit markets.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 IRAQ SEEKS AN ALTERNATIVE TO HORMUZ

Chevron and Iraq are discussing a potential pipeline network that could transport Iraqi oil northwards and then west through Syria to the Mediterranean.

The project could partly revive older pipelines and create export routes through Syria, Turkey or Jordan.

Iraq normally depends heavily on the Strait of Hormuz and has reportedly been forced to reduce production sharply because of the disruption.

Simple explanation

Iraq currently relies heavily on one narrow shipping route.

A pipeline to the Mediterranean would create another exit for its oil.

Why it matters

The proposal reveals a major long-term consequence of the war:

Middle Eastern producers may spend heavily to reduce their dependence on vulnerable maritime chokepoints.

That could benefit pipeline operators, engineering companies and countries positioned along alternative energy corridors.

However, construction, financing and security risks mean the project remains uncertain and would take years to complete.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 UBER MAKES A $14.8 BILLION DELIVERY BET

Uber has agreed to acquire Delivery Hero in a transaction valuing the German company’s equity at $14.8 billion, subject to regulatory and shareholder approval.

The combination would create the largest food-delivery group outside China and significantly expand Uber’s international reach.

Simple explanation

Uber is buying one of its biggest competitors instead of continuing to fight it market by market.

A larger network may lower costs and allow Uber to sell rides, food delivery and subscriptions to the same customers.

Why it matters

The deal shows that scale has become increasingly important in delivery businesses, where thin margins make it difficult for smaller companies to compete.

It may also face intense regulatory scrutiny because the combined company would hold powerful positions in numerous markets.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 ELI LILLY MAKES A MAJOR BET ON PSYCHEDELIC MEDICINE

Eli Lilly agreed to acquire AtaiBeckley for an initial $2.8 billion, with another $1 billion potentially payable if development milestones are achieved.

AtaiBeckley is developing psychedelic-based treatments for mental-health conditions.

Simple explanation

Lilly is paying a premium for experimental medicines that may offer new ways to treat illnesses such as depression.

The treatments remain risky because they still need to prove they are safe, effective and commercially viable.

Why it matters

The acquisition is an important vote of confidence from one of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies.

It suggests psychedelic medicine is moving from a speculative niche towards mainstream pharmaceutical investment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 CHINA CHALLENGES THE US OVER GLOBAL AI LEADERSHIP

Twenty-nine countries have signed an agreement establishing the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization, based in Shanghai.

Chinese President Xi Jinping presented China as a supporter of more open and internationally shared AI development, particularly for emerging economies.

Simple explanation

The competition over AI is no longer only about building the strongest model.

It is also about deciding the international rules, standards and infrastructure that other countries will use.

Why it matters

The world may gradually develop competing AI ecosystems:

🔸 a US-led system focussed on controlled supply chains and security

🔸 a China-led system emphasising access, sovereignty and development

Countries may eventually face pressure to choose between technologies, standards and suppliers from rival blocs.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 DEEPSEEK’S IMPLIED VALUE REACHES $52 BILLION

A Chinese regulatory filing implied that DeepSeek was valued at approximately $52 billion during a private transaction.

The figure is not an official valuation announced by the company, but it provides the clearest public evidence yet of how investors are valuing the Chinese AI developer.

Why it matters

DeepSeek’s valuation shows that investors believe lower-cost Chinese AI models could become serious global competitors.

It also reinforces the central challenge facing the AI market:

the cost of developing frontier models is rising rapidly, while the useful life of any individual technological advantage may be becoming shorter.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 CRYPTO: ETF DEMAND REMAINS MIXED

US spot cryptocurrency ETFs recorded mixed flows on 16 July:

🔸 Bitcoin: approximately $79.2 million of net inflows

🔸 Ethereum: approximately $28 million of net outflows

🔸 XRP: approximately $6.8 million of inflows

🔸 Solana: approximately $1.7 million of inflows

Simple explanation

More money entered Bitcoin, XRP and Solana investment funds than left them, while Ethereum funds experienced withdrawals.

Why it matters

Bitcoin continues to attract institutional demand, but crypto remains vulnerable to the same forces affecting other risk assets:

🔸 higher interest-rate expectations

🔸 rising oil and inflation risk

🔸 a stronger dollar

🔸 declining appetite for speculative investments

ETF inflows may provide support, but they do not make crypto immune to a broader market deleveraging event.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 WHAT IT MEANS

Today’s market is sending eight important messages.

1️⃣ The AI trade is being repriced—not disproved

The long-term importance of artificial intelligence has not disappeared.

But investors are questioning whether every AI-linked company deserves the extreme valuation created during the rally.

2️⃣ Excellent earnings may already be reflected in prices

TSMC and ASML delivered strong results, yet their shares weakened.

That is a warning that expectations—not merely profits—determine short-term market reactions.

3️⃣ Leverage can turn an ordinary correction into a violent sell-off

Options, leveraged funds and momentum strategies amplify gains on the way up.

They can amplify losses when the direction reverses.

4️⃣ Market breadth remains the key dividing line

Financials, healthcare, consumer staples, retailers and transports are holding up better.

As long as capital rotates rather than disappears, the broader bull market may remain intact.

5️⃣ Oil is the bridge between war and monetary policy

The conflict matters to investors because disrupted energy supplies can raise inflation.

Higher inflation can keep interest rates elevated or force the Fed to tighten again.

6️⃣ The Fed is no longer discussing only rate cuts

A growing group of policymakers believes rates may not be restrictive enough.

This creates a more difficult environment for expensive, long-duration growth assets.

7️⃣ The global AI race is becoming geopolitical

Chinese models, capital and governance institutions are challenging American dominance.

AI is becoming both an investment theme and a struggle over international influence.

8️⃣ Selectivity matters more than direction

This is no longer a market where buying every AI-related company produces easy gains.

Investors must distinguish between:

🔸 strong technology and strong economics

🔸 revenue growth and profitable growth

🔸 an exceptional company and an attractive valuation

🔸 a temporary correction and a broken investment thesis

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

MARKET OUTLOOK

The immediate market risk remains tilted lower while semiconductor selling continues.

The most important signals to watch are:

🔸 whether the PHLX Semiconductor Index crosses the 20% bear-market threshold

🔸 whether selling spreads from chips into financials, healthcare and defensives

🔸 whether the VIX rises decisively above 20

🔸 whether oil holds above the mid-$80s or moves towards $90

🔸 whether the US 10-year Treasury yield remains near 4.5% despite hawkish Fed remarks

🔸 whether leveraged investors begin liquidating unrelated assets

🔸 whether US–Iran attacks cause lasting damage to Hormuz or Red Sea shipping

For now, this remains a severe technology and momentum correction inside a broader market that is still functioning.

But the combination of leverage, expensive valuations, war-driven oil inflation and hawkish central-bank rhetoric means investors should not treat the sell-off casually.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

“The greatest investment opportunities often appear when a powerful long-term trend meets a painful short-term correction—but patience means waiting until price and value finally meet.”

JPMorgan Chase: U.S.

stock market deleveraging has not yet ended and will still face volatility pressure in the coming months JPMorgan Chase said in its latest research report that the investor deleveraging process that began in June is still continuing, and there is room for further leverage reduction in leveraged stock ETFs, options and financing accounts, and is expected to become an important headwind for the stock market in the coming months.

Hedge funds have taken the lead in showing signs of reducing leverage. Although the S&P 500 and Nasdaq fell in June, equity long-short funds and technology, media and telecommunications funds still rose 1.2% and 3.7% respectively for the month, mainly supported by semiconductor stocks. During the same period, semiconductor ETFs rose by 9.5%, while U.S.

ultra-large-scale cloud computing companies fell by 14.5%, showing that related funds were still overweighting semiconductors. However, high-frequency data in July showed that the correlation between hedge funds and semiconductor stock trends has weakened, and their leverage indicators have also fallen from the highs set in June since 2017.

The risks of leveraged ETFs have not yet been fully released. Since peaking in June, the asset size of memory chip leveraged ETFs has dropped by 34%, and that of all stock leveraged ETFs has dropped by 13%, but the ratio relative to the underlying market value is still significantly higher than the level before April.

JP Morgan predicts that the market may need about three months of consolidation before the above ratio returns to normal. The ratio of memory chip leveraged ETF assets to underlying market capitalization is about three times that of the overall stock leveraged ETF, but leverage risks have spread to the broader stock market.

Retail options trading is also cooling off. The number of net call options bought by small customers was close to 14 million on June 5, which was equivalent to the highs in November 2021 and October 2025, and has since fallen significantly.

Historically, when this indicator drops to 2 million to 4 million, it often corresponds to the end of the adjustment of technology stocks. Meanwhile, net borrowing in the NYSE funding account remains at historically high levels, with only tentative signs of decline.

JPMorgan Chase believes that short-term deleveraging will dominate the market and increase price volatility, but long-term capital supply and demand can still provide support. The bank estimates that global stock demand will be approximately US$475 billion in 2026.

After deducting approximately US$200 billion in new supply, the net demand will be approximately US$275 billion, including approximately US$200 billion in the second half of the year.

Retail investors may have inflows of US$1.03 trillion throughout the year, with approximately US$480 billion still flowing in in the second half of the year, but pension and insurance institutions are expected to sell approximately US$235 billion in net sales during the same period. Additionally, Bitcoin futures are sending positive signals.

Despite repeated spot Bitcoin ETF capital flows, CME futures and perpetual contract positions have improved, and Strategy's U.S. dollar reserves have also increased from $2.55 billion to $3 billion, equivalent to about 20 months of dividend payments, helping to ease its concerns about selling Bitcoin in the future to raise funds.

Goldman Sachs: The European Central Bank is unlikely to raise interest rates in July, and further tightening is still the mainstream expectation Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest research report that the resumption of military conflicts in the Middle East and the renewed rise in energy prices have led ECB officials to generally believe that inflation risks are still upward.

However, an immediate increase in interest rates in July is not the current policy option, and policymakers are more likely to remain on the sidelines, waiting for energy market trends and the latest economic forecast in September.

Schnabel, executive member of the European Central Bank, said the current shock cannot be simply ignored because it has produced indirect inflationary pressures and may bring about second-round effects. Bundesbank President Nagel advocated remaining cautious about rising energy prices while retaining room to take decisive action when necessary.

Austrian Central Bank President Koch stressed that the European Central Bank will take necessary measures to ensure that inflation falls back to 2% in the medium term. However, no ECB official has explicitly called for a rate hike in July.

Some policymakers believe that as oil and gas prices have fallen from their highs, the urgency for successive interest rate hikes has diminished. Belgian central bank governor Wensch said that if the conflict is brought under control and energy prices continue to fall, the case for further tightening of policy will weaken.

European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde also said that risks are more balanced than a few weeks ago and future decisions will still depend on the data. Goldman Sachs pointed out that although the probability of action in July is low, most officials still believe that the tightening cycle is not over yet.

Schnabel, Slovenia Central Bank Governor Dolenc, Estonia Central Bank Governor Kasik and Malta Central Bank Governor De Marco all hinted that at least one more interest rate increase is still necessary. Kasik said it is reasonable for the market to expect another interest rate hike to ensure inflation returns to 2%.

At the same time, policymakers have yet to see clear signs of a wage-price spiral or runaway inflation expectations. Both Koch and Bank of Finland Governor Rehn said that there is currently no significant second-round effect and long-term inflation expectations remain anchored, but the economic growth outlook remains fragile.

The latest analysis by ECB staff also raised the neutral interest rate range from the previous 1.75% to 2.25% to 1.75% to 2.50%.

Lagarde emphasized that this range will not directly determine recent policy; Chief Economist Lane said that after the energy shock subsides, this range will affect the final interest rate level of this round of interest rate hike cycles.

Judging from historical experience, the three bear markets experienced by gold since 1970 all gave back at least 50% of the previous gains. The 50% Fibonacci retracement level of this rally is around $3,702, which Bank of America views as a key target in a deep correction. Another model based on 174 weeks of historical data points to $3,605.

It plans to enter mass production in 2028. Goldman Sachs believes that the above trends are positive for ASML and ASM International. ASML previously planned to increase low numerical aperture EUV equipment production capacity by 30% in 2027 and 2028 respectively.

As the energy efficiency and bandwidth requirements of AI data centers increase, the demand for TSMC's COUPE packaging technology is expected to grow, which will also promote the application of BESI hybrid bonding equipment.

In addition, TSMC will continue to expand its mature process capacity for AI-related power management chips and CMOS image sensors, providing demand verification for the power semiconductor businesses of Infineon and STMicroelectronics.

However, the company also warned that rising component costs are putting pressure on consumer electronics and price-sensitive markets.

Goldman Sachs gave ASML, ASM International, BESI and Infineon a "buy" rating, with target prices of 2,200 euros, 955 euros, 318 euros and 88 euros respectively; it maintained a "neutral" rating on STMicroelectronics, with a target price of 58 euros.

Goldman Sachs: AI data centers will generate demand for behind-the-meter energy storage, and deployment in the United States will increase to 172GWh in 2030 Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest research report that the accelerated construction of artificial intelligence data centers and the increase in single cabinet power density are promoting behind-the-meter battery energy storage systems (BTM BESS) to become an important solution to solve power supply bottlenecks.

The energy storage system has the advantages of fast deployment, millisecond-level response, backup power supply and improved power quality. It can serve as a transitional power supply before the completion of grid expansion and cooperate with on-site power generation for a long time.

The scale of traditional data centers has expanded from 5 to 10 megawatts to 100 to 200 megawatts, and some AI parks have reached the gigawatt level. The power density of cabinets has also increased from 5 to 10 kilowatts to more than 50 to 100 kilowatts. GPU loads can rise from zero to full power in milliseconds.

Gas turbines are suitable for stable base loads, but are difficult to adjust frequently. Energy storage projects can usually be deployed within 12 to 18 months after contracting, bypassing the wait for grid connection that may last 4 to 8 years. Goldman Sachs raised its forecast for the compound annual growth rate of U.S.

electricity demand from 2.6% to 3.2%, of which demand on the grid side will grow by 2.8% and behind-the-meter power supply will contribute the remaining 0.5 percentage points, equivalent to approximately 20 GW of new capacity by 2030, which is approximately 14 GW based on a 70% capacity factor. The bank estimates that U.S.

data center behind-the-table energy storage demand will be about 60 GWh in 2030, pushing U.S. energy storage deployment up from the previous forecast of 112 GWh to 172 GWh, of which approximately 161 GWh are utility-scale projects.

Goldman Sachs also raised its forecast for energy storage deployment in Europe, the Middle East and Africa in 2030 from 83 GWh to 142 GWh, and more than doubled Australia's forecast for 2026 to 2028 due to policy support. Global energy storage installed capacity is expected to grow by 30% in 2027, reaching approximately 2,100 GWh by 2040.

Goldman Sachs: AI demand visibility extends to 2030, TSMC expands production to consolidate foundry leadership Goldman Sachs said in its latest research report that TSMC’s second-quarter results further verified that the demand for AI continues to increase.

The company has raised its full-year revenue and capital expenditure guidance, and accelerated the expansion of advanced processes and advanced packaging. Its industry leading position will not be significantly shaken in the next few years.

TSMC raised its guidance for US dollar-denominated revenue growth in 2026 from "more than 30%" to "just over 40%", which is higher than Goldman Sachs' previous forecast of 39%; its full-year capital expenditure was raised from the high end of the range of US$52 billion to US$56 billion to US$60 billion to US$64 billion.

Management said that customers have continued to release strong demand signals extending from 2029 to 2030. The development trajectory of AI is stronger than previously expected. Smart AI will also drive demand for various types of CPUs such as x86, ARM and RISC-V.

Second-quarter revenue was US$40.2 billion, a 12% month-on-month increase and a year-on-year increase of 34%, which was at the upper edge of guidance; gross profit margin was 67.7%, higher than the company's guidance, but lower than Goldman Sachs's forecast of 68.5%; operating profit margin was 60.3%, and earnings per share was NT$27.25, about 12% higher than Goldman Sachs' forecast.

The revenue guidance for the third quarter is US$44.6 billion to US$45.8 billion, with a median growth of 12% quarter-on-quarter; the gross profit margin is expected to be 65% to 67%. Although N2 mass production will drag down the gross profit margin in the second half of the year by 300 to 400 basis points, the actual decline may be smaller than expected.

TSMC plans to invest an additional US$100 billion in Arizona, bringing the cumulative local committed investment to approximately US$265 billion, and will add four additional wafer fabs based on customer demand.

Goldman Sachs estimates that the company's capital expenditures from 2026 to 2028 will be US$64 billion, US$78 billion, and US$82 billion respectively. N2 will be rapidly ramped up in the second half of 2026, and A14 is planned to be mass produced in 2028.

Goldman Sachs believes that the rising costs of equipment and overseas factory construction will support TSMC's gradual price increases. Price increases are expected to be low to mid-single digits in 2027, and gross profit margins will reach 66.9%, 67.0% and 67.5% respectively from 2026 to 2028.

The demand for mature processes continues to diverge, the supply of AI-related power management chips and sensors is tight, and the demand for consumer electronics is still weak.

Goldman Sachs raised TSMC's earnings per share forecast from 2026 to 2028 by 6.0%, 3.4%, and 3.2% respectively, maintaining a "buy" rating, and raised the target price of Taiwan stocks from NT$3,000 to NT$3,100, and the ADR target price from US$600 to US$620.

Goldman Sachs: Netflix’s Q2 results are basically in line with expectations, and interaction and capital allocation still suppress market sentiment Goldman Sachs predicts that the market may react negatively after Netflix’s second-quarter earnings report is released.

Although revenue was basically in line with expectations and operating profit was slightly better than expected, investors' attention is still focused on two major issues: user interaction and capital allocation.

Netflix's second-quarter revenue increased 13% year-on-year to US$12.56 billion, an increase of 12% at constant exchange rates, slightly lower than Goldman Sachs' forecast of US$12.58 billion.

In terms of regions, revenue in the United States and Canada increased by 10% to US$5.43 billion, Europe, the Middle East and Africa increased by 14% to US$4.03 billion, Latin America increased by 21% to US$1.58 billion, and Asia-Pacific increased by 16% to US$1.51 billion.

GAAP operating profit for the quarter was US$4.19 billion, higher than market expectations of US$4.12 billion; operating profit margin reached 33.4%, higher than the company's previous guidance of 32.6%; earnings per share was US$0.80, also slightly higher than expected. However, Netflix's guidance for the third quarter is weak.

The company expects revenue of US$12.86 billion, a year-on-year increase of 12%, lower than market expectations of US$13.01 billion; operating profit of US$4.27 billion, lower than market expectations of US$4.38 billion; earnings per share is expected to be US$0.82, lower than market expectations of US$0.85.

Netflix also narrowed its full-year revenue guidance for 2026 from US$50.7 billion to US$51.7 billion to US$51 billion to US$51.4 billion, and maintained an operating profit margin target of 31.5%. Goldman Sachs believes that the market may react negatively after Netflix’s second-quarter earnings report is released.

Iraq has signed preliminary agreements worth more than $60 billion with US and British companies across the energy, healthcare and technology sectors, as Baghdad seeks Western investment and alternative routes for exporting its oil. The deals were announced at a US-Iraq business summit in Washington, where Iraqi Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi said his government was pursuing an “open-door policy” for foreign investors.

A central project involves restoring the long-defunct pipeline linking Iraq’s oil-producing Kirkuk region to Syria’s Mediterranean port of Baniyas. The route could initially transport up to two million barrels of crude per day, allowing Iraq to bypass the Strait of Hormuz following Iran’s effective closure of the waterway in response to the US-Israeli war on Iran. The closure resulted in Iraqi crude exports plunging by as much as 90 percent, placing severe strain on Baghdad’s finances, with oil revenues accounting for the vast majority of state funding.

The wider $60 billion package includes Chevron agreements related to the pipeline route and expanded production at the West Qurna 2 and Nassiriya fields, while ConocoPhillips plans to acquire a 42 percent stake in a BP subsidiary managing four northern Iraqi oilfields. Baghdad has also reached an agreement allowing Elon Musk’s Starlink to operate in Iraq.
